structure: Most creators say {common_advice}. Here’s what actually works for {niche}: {specific_action} (and the proof is in the results).
notes: High retention when the first clause contrasts common advice vs a specific action; proof cue must be delivered visually.

structure: Quick interrupt: {mistake_or_problem}. Do this instead in 10 seconds: {micro_fix}. Save this for your next post.
notes: Works best when micro_fix is concrete and repeatable; avoid vague reassurance.

structure: Setup (1 line): Here’s the context for {niche}. Shift (1 line): the reason your results plateau. Proof (2 lines): what changed + mini screenshot/metric. Next step (1 line): one action viewers can do today.
notes: Strong for “consistency growth” creators; proof beats generic motivational lines.

structure: I tested {tool/approach} on {niche} for 7 days. Day 1 vs Day 7: {before} → {after}. Here’s the exact script I used.
notes: Time-boxing + before/after framing boosts curiosity and saves.

structure: Comment “{keyword}” and I’ll send the template for {niche} (tomorrow).
notes: Engagement CTA feels trustworthy when paired with a useful artifact.

structure: Don’t scroll—save this. If you post about {niche} next week, reuse this hook structure.
notes: Save CTA should come after the hook promise is clarified; avoid “save” in line 1.

structure: Stop doing {common_action} if your goal is {goal}. Try this exact sequence: {sequence}.
notes: Command phrasing increases CTR; ensure sequence is clear and not overly aggressive.

structure: Pattern: Problem (what most people do wrong) → Shift (what to do instead) → Proof (mini evidence) → One action (comment/save/follow). Keep each beat under 2 seconds.
notes: Beat pacing constraint makes scripts feel native and improves comprehension.

structure: Here’s the curiosity gap: {belief} vs {reality}. The 1 change that bridges them is {one_change}.
notes: Curiosity gap phrasing consistently improves retention when reality is specific.

structure: Story hook: I almost quit on {niche}… then I changed {habit} and got {result}. Here’s the exact checklist I followed.
notes: Use emotional “almost quit” only if followed by a real checklist (not vague inspiration).

structure: If this helps, follow—next I’ll show {next_topic} for {niche}.
notes: CTA feels less salesy when it promises the next teaching beat.

structure: For TikTok Shop: The product solves {problem}—here’s the exact demo moment (skip to 0:12).
notes: Shop CTA must reference a demo moment; otherwise viewers feel sold-to.

structure: Reply with your niche + current setup, and I’ll suggest a hook variant to test next.
notes: Best for service creators; makes CTA interactive and lowers resistance.

structure: Hashtag set: #{niche} + #{sub_niche} + #howto + #tips + #creatortok + #fyp (only if hook matches).
notes: Keep one discovery tag (#fyp/#foryou) gated by relevance; prefer instructional tags.

structure: Hashtag set: one branded tag + three niche tags + two intent tags (e.g. #hook #tutorial). Avoid generic spam tags.
notes: Brand tag keeps consistency; intent tags improve click/save matching.

structure: I tried {approach} for {niche} — here’s what changed (in {time_box}).
notes: Time-boxing adds clarity; keep promise aligned with the first 3 seconds.

structure: {3_mistakes} Mistakes Killing Your {niche} (and the fix).
notes: Use “fix” in the title so the payoff is obvious; avoid fear-mongering.

structure: Stop doing {common_action} if you want {goal} — do this instead.
notes: Command style boosts CTR; must remain constructive to avoid backlash.

structure: Hook variants A/B (curiosity vs proof): Generate 2 hooks, then use the winning beat’s exact wording in the caption opener.
notes: This explicit A/B instruction reduces generic output and improves learning loop.

structure: CTA timing guard: if goal is reach/followers, delay any “buy/product” language until after proof. Use save/comment/DM CTA first.
notes: Prevents sales-heavy first lines; often improves retention before conversion.
